key_insight: The paper shows that realistic facial wrinkles under contact can be generated by combining viscoelastic skin mechanics with anatomically constrained attachments that shape wrinkle amplitude, wavelength, and placement.
engineering_relevance: This provides a physically based FEM tool for believable facial simulation in graphics, animation, and digital humans where contact-driven skin detail matters.
method: Model skin as a viscoelastic layered material with time-dependent relaxation.
Use high-order prismatic solid-shell finite elements to capture through-thickness stress and fine wrinkles.
Introduce continuum-based skin ligaments to represent heterogeneous facial attachments and mobility constraints.
evidence_signal: The method was evaluated on synthetic examples and compared against real-world footage, showing temporally coherent and visually realistic wrinkle patterns during transient contact.
watch_item: The approach likely requires careful parameterization and high computational cost to resolve high-frequency wrinkling and anatomically detailed ligament behavior.
paper_title: Physics-Based Simulation of Contact-Induced Facial Wrinkling
